answers.value: Most Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays I am available between 9:00 a.m. and 3:00 p.m. On Saturdays I am typically available from 10:00 a.m. to 1:00 p.m. I am flexible to adjust slightly around these times with advance notice, especially for special events or holidays.

answers.field_label: Why Are You Interested In Volunteering At The Waters
answers.value:
I am interested in volunteering at The Waters because I have always felt a strong connection with older adults, and I am at a point in my life where I have both the time and emotional capacity to contribute in a meaningful, consistent way. My children are grown and out of the house, and I recently transitioned from full-time work as a part-time occupational therapy assistant in an outpatient clinic to semi-retirement. I miss the day-to-day interactions and relationships I built with older patients, but I do not necessarily want to return to a full clinical schedule. Volunteering at a senior living community feels like a natural and fulfilling next step.

On a personal level, my grandmother lived in an assisted living community in Wisconsin for the last eight years of her life. I visited her frequently, and I saw firsthand how much of a difference a kind volunteer could make in her day. Something as simple as someone sitting to play a game of cards with her, helping her write a birthday card to a great-grandchild, or walking with her to look at the flowers outside completely changed her mood and sense of connection. I remember one volunteer who used to come on Tuesday mornings and read the newspaper aloud to a small group of residents who had vision issues; my grandmother talked about those mornings all week long. That experience really stayed with me and shaped how I think about community and dignity in aging.

Professionally, I have worked with adults and seniors for more than 15 years as an occupational therapy assistant. While I know I wouldn’t be volunteering in a clinical capacity, that background has made me very comfortable around mobility devices, hearing and vision challenges, memory changes, and the slower pace that many residents need. I understand how important it is to give people the time and space to express themselves, to maintain as much independence as possible, and to feel respected rather than rushed. I think that perspective will help me be a calm, patient, and reliable presence for residents and staff.

I am also drawn specifically to The Waters because of your emphasis on whole-person well-being and community life. I spent some time on your website reading about your life enrichment programming, and it really resonated with me that you focus not just on physical needs but also on social, emotional, and spiritual wellness. I would love to support group activities like art, music, simple exercise, and conversation circles, as well as quieter one-on-one visits with residents who may not be able or willing to join larger groups.

In addition, I want to be intentional about how I use this phase of my life. I have more flexible time now, and I do not want that time to be spent only on myself. I get a lot of joy from listening to people’s life stories—what they did for work, how they raised their families, what they are proud of, and what they find funny or meaningful. I have found that seniors often appreciate having someone who is not in a rush and who genuinely wants to hear about their memories, even if those stories are repeated. I believe that by showing up consistently, learning residents’ names and preferences, and being a familiar face on certain days of the week, I can contribute to a sense of stability and companionship for the people who live at The Waters.

Finally, I am hoping that volunteering will be mutually enriching. I know I will be giving my time and energy, but I also expect to learn a great deal—from residents, from staff, and from the experience of being in a community where people are at a later stage of life. I have found that spending time with older adults helps me keep my own life in perspective and reminds me of what truly matters: relationships, kindness, small daily joys, and humor. My goal is to become a dependable, long-term volunteer who can be counted on to show up, be present, and help residents feel seen, valued, and connected to the world around them.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Areas Of Interest With Residents Skills And Previous Experience Volunteering With Seniors
answers.value:
Areas of interest with residents:
- One-on-one friendly visits: I really enjoy sitting with residents in their rooms or in quieter common areas to talk, look through photo albums, or simply provide company while they knit, do puzzles, or rest. I am comfortable with silence and do not mind if a resident repeats stories or needs extra time to find their words.
- Small group activities: I am especially interested in helping with small group activities like card games (Go Fish, Rummy, Uno), simple board games, bingo, and trivia. I find that these types of activities work well for varying cognitive and physical abilities, and they can be easily adapted so that everyone can participate at their own level.
- Arts and crafts: I enjoy simple arts and crafts projects such as making seasonal decorations, painting with watercolors, adult coloring pages, and making greeting cards. I am not an artist, but I am very good at organizing materials, giving gentle encouragement, and helping people feel successful with whatever they create.
- Reading and writing support: I would love to read aloud to residents—short stories, newspaper articles, poems, or parts of books—especially for those with vision challenges. I can also help residents write letters or cards to family members, or help them send simple emails if appropriate.
- Light exercise and walks: Weather and safety permitting, I enjoy accompanying residents on short walks in hallways or in safe outdoor areas, pushing wheelchairs if needed and approved, and encouraging gentle movement. I am comfortable walking at a slow pace, allowing rest breaks, and watching for signs of fatigue.
- Music and reminiscence: I like to bring in music from different eras (especially 1940s–1970s) and either listen together, sing along, or use the songs as a starting point for conversation about memories. I do not play an instrument, but I can help set up music on a tablet or speaker and lead simple sing-alongs.

Relevant skills:
- Strong communication and listening skills: In my previous work as an occupational therapy assistant, I spent a great deal of time talking with older adults, explaining activities clearly, and listening carefully to their concerns and preferences. I am patient, speak clearly, and naturally slow my pace when needed without being condescending.
- Comfort with mobility and adaptive equipment: I am very familiar with walkers, wheelchairs, canes, grab bars, and other mobility aids. While I understand that as a volunteer I would follow your specific policies and not perform any tasks outside my role, my comfort level around this equipment helps me remain calm and reassuring when residents are moving from place to place.
- Experience with dementia and memory loss: I have worked with many individuals living with mild to moderate dementia. I understand the importance of maintaining a calm tone, avoiding arguing or correcting unnecessarily, and meeting people where they are in the moment. I am used to repeating information gently, redirecting conversations when someone becomes distressed, and focusing on feelings rather than strict facts.
- Organization and reliability: I am detail-oriented and punctual. I keep a paper planner and a digital calendar and take commitments seriously. In past volunteer roles, I was often the person who arrived early to help set up, stayed to clean up, and communicated clearly if I ever needed to miss a scheduled time.
- Basic infection control awareness: Through my healthcare background, I am used to routine hand hygiene, wearing masks when appropriate, and following facility rules about illness and visitation. I understand how important this is in a senior living environment.

Previous experience volunteering with seniors:
- Church visitation ministry (2016–2023): For about seven years, I volunteered through my church’s visitation team. I was paired with three homebound older adults at different times, visiting each person about twice a month. My visits typically involved bringing printed church bulletins, chatting about their week, reading short devotional passages if they were interested, and sometimes helping them make phone calls to family members. One of the women I visited, Helen, had early-stage dementia; I learned how to gently redirect her when she became fixated on worries (for example, thinking she needed to go to work even though she had been retired for decades).
- Assisted living activity helper (2019–2021): Before the pandemic, I volunteered at a small assisted living community in Edina approximately once a week. I primarily helped the activities director with bingo, birthday celebrations, and seasonal events (Valentine’s Day parties, fall pumpkin decorating, etc.). My responsibilities included setting up tables and chairs, bringing residents from their rooms to the common area, offering refreshments, calling out bingo numbers, and then escorting residents back afterward. I also occasionally did one-on-one visits with residents who chose not to attend group activities, simply sitting with them, listening, or reading aloud.
- Senior center technology helper (2022–2024): More recently, I volunteered at a local senior center one afternoon a week helping older adults with basic technology questions—how to use email, how to join a Zoom call with family, how to enlarge the text on their phones, etc. Many of the people I helped were in their 70s and 80s. This experience taught me to break tasks down into small, manageable steps and to be very patient and encouraging, especially when someone felt embarrassed about not understanding a device.

Overall, I feel very comfortable in senior environments and around people with a wide range of abilities. I am not easily flustered, I am happy to repeat myself or slow down as needed, and I genuinely enjoy building ongoing relationships with the same residents over time. I would be grateful for any training or orientation you provide so that I can align my skills and interests with the specific needs and policies of The Waters.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
